title: Do Howdens Fit Their Own Kitchens?
metaDescription: Howdens does not offer a consumer installation service. Find out why and how to find a trusted independent kitchen fitter for your Howdens kitchen.
answer: No. Howdens is a trade-only supplier — it sells to builders and kitchen fitters, not directly to homeowners. Howdens does not provide an installation service. You will need to arrange your own independent kitchen fitter.
bodyHtml:
Why Howdens Doesn't Install

Howdens operates on a trade-only model. Their depots supply to professional kitchen fitters and builders who then manage the installation for their clients. This keeps their pricing competitive and their business focused on supply.

How to Find a Fitter for Your Howdens Kitchen

You can ask your Howdens depot for recommendations, or find an independent installer directly. Look for fitters with proven Howdens experience — the unit assembly method, fixing patterns and door adjustment systems are specific to Howdens and benefit from hands-on familiarity.

What to Check Before Booking a Fitter

  • Ask whether they have fitted Howdens kitchens before
  • Request a site survey before any quote is given
  • Confirm the quote covers all cabinets, worktops, plinths and integrated appliances
  • Check that the fixed price includes snagging at completion

faqs.q: Can Howdens recommend a fitter?
faqs.a: Yes, Howdens depots often have a list of local fitters they work with regularly. Ask at the depot when you place your order.

faqs.q: Do I need a builder or a kitchen fitter for Howdens?
faqs.a: A specialist kitchen fitter is usually the right choice unless structural work is also needed. Kitchen fitters have the specific skills for unit assembly, door alignment and worktop fitting.

faqs.q: Is it expensive to get a Howdens kitchen fitted independently?
faqs.a: Installation costs for a Howdens kitchen typically range from £1,200 to £3,500 depending on kitchen size and complexity. A pre-installation survey will give you an accurate fixed price.

title: Is Wren Kitchen Installation Worth It?
metaDescription: Is Wren's own installation service worth the cost? We compare Wren installation vs independent fitters to help you make the right choice.
answer: Wren offers its own installation service, but many customers find that independent kitchen fitters offer better value, more personalised service, and greater accountability. Whether Wren installation is worth it depends on your priorities.
bodyHtml:
What Wren Installation Includes

Wren's installation service is sold as a package with the kitchen. It typically covers fitting of all carcasses, doors, worktops and integrated appliances. Surveys are conducted by a Wren representative.

The Case for Independent Fitters

Independent fitters tend to offer more flexibility, a personal point of accountability, and often more competitive pricing. An independent fitter is not incentivised to upsell additional services and will give you a fixed price based solely on what your kitchen requires.

Key Differences

  • Independent fitters conduct their own survey, often more thoroughly
  • Fixed pricing from independent fitters tends to be clearer
  • You deal directly with the person doing the work — no third-party subcontracting
  • Independent fitters are not limited by retailer-set timescales

faqs.q: Does Wren use subcontractors for installation?
faqs.a: Wren typically uses a network of approved installation contractors rather than directly employed fitters. You may not meet your fitter until the day of installation.

faqs.q: Can I refuse Wren installation and use my own fitter?
faqs.a: Yes. Purchasing a Wren kitchen does not obligate you to use their installation service. You can use any qualified independent kitchen fitter.

faqs.q: Will using an independent fitter void my Wren warranty?
faqs.a: No. The warranty on Wren kitchen units relates to the product, not who installs it. A competent independent fitter will not affect your product warranty.

title: Can IKEA Kitchens Be Professionally Installed?
metaDescription: Yes, IKEA kitchens can be professionally installed by independent kitchen fitters. Find out what to expect and how to get a fixed-price installation quote.
answer: Yes. IKEA kitchens can be professionally installed by independent kitchen fitters. IKEA also offers its own installation service via Taskrabbit (in some areas), but many homeowners choose independent specialists for a more personalised, accountable service.
bodyHtml:
IKEA's Own Installation Service

IKEA has partnered with Taskrabbit in some areas to offer an installation service. However, availability varies by location and the service is managed through a third-party platform rather than directly by IKEA.

Why Choose an Independent Fitter for IKEA

Independent kitchen fitters with IKEA experience understand the SEKTION and METOD unit systems, including how to handle corner carousels, manage appliance integration and ensure the finished kitchen is level on real-world floors. A pre-installation survey from a specialist will catch problems before they arise.

What to Expect

  • IKEA kitchens are flatpack systems — assembly time is factored into the installation
  • Pre-installation surveys are essential as IKEA plans are sometimes drawn with approximate dimensions
  • Worktop cutting and fitting, plinth installation and appliance connection are included with a full installation service

faqs.q: Is IKEA kitchen installation difficult?
faqs.a: IKEA kitchens require careful assembly and precise levelling. An experienced kitchen fitter will handle this efficiently and to a professional standard.

faqs.q: How much does it cost to have an IKEA kitchen professionally installed?
faqs.a: Professional installation of an IKEA kitchen typically costs between £1,000 and £3,000 depending on the number of units and complexity of the job.

faqs.q: Does IKEA kitchen installation include the worktop?
faqs.a: If you are having a specialist kitchen fitter, worktop templating, cutting and fitting is usually included. Confirm this when booking your survey.

title: Who Measures for Quartz Worktops?
metaDescription: Quartz worktops require precise templating before cutting. Find out who measures for quartz worktops and how the templating process works.
answer: Quartz worktops are measured by a specialist templating company, usually sent by the worktop supplier, after your kitchen units have been fitted and levelled. The template is then used to cut the quartz slab precisely before it is returned for fitting.
bodyHtml:
The Templating Process

Once your kitchen carcasses are installed and levelled, a worktop templating technician visits your home. They use a laser or physical template to record exact measurements of every section of worktop required. This template is sent to the fabrication facility where the quartz is cut to size.

Why Templating Happens After Unit Fitting

Quartz cannot be adjusted on site. It must be cut exactly to the dimensions of your fitted units. Any error in measurement means a costly replacement. For this reason, templating always follows unit installation — never before.

Who Arranges the Template Visit?

  • Usually the worktop supplier arranges the template visit
  • Your kitchen fitter will confirm when the units are ready for templating
  • Template-to-delivery turnaround is typically 7–14 days

faqs.q: Can my kitchen fitter measure for quartz?
faqs.a: Some kitchen fitters have templating experience, but most quartz suppliers send their own specialist templaters to ensure accuracy and maintain their warranty.

faqs.q: How long after kitchen fitting can quartz be templated?
faqs.a: Templating can usually take place the day after units are fitted and confirmed level. There is no minimum wait period.

faqs.q: What if the quartz measurements are wrong?
faqs.a: If the worktop supplier made an error in cutting, they are responsible for replacement. This is why professional templating — rather than self-measurement — is standard.

title: What Happens at a Kitchen Survey?
metaDescription: A kitchen survey is the essential first step before installation. Find out exactly what happens at a pre-installation kitchen survey and why it matters.
answer: At a kitchen survey, a kitchen installation specialist visits your home to assess the room, check your kitchen plans against the real dimensions, identify any structural or service issues, and provide a fixed installation quote.
bodyHtml:
What the Fitter Checks During a Survey

A thorough kitchen survey covers the room dimensions (laser measured), floor levels, wall plumb and square conditions, positions of existing services (water supply, waste, gas, electrics), ceiling height and any obstructions such as chimney breasts or boxed-in pipework.

Plan Review

The surveyor will also review your kitchen designer's plans and cross-check them against the actual room. Discrepancies between plan and reality are common — and catching them at survey stage prevents expensive problems during installation.

What You Receive After the Survey

  • A fixed installation price — itemised by scope
  • A recommended installation timeline
  • Notes on any preparatory work required before installation begins
  • Advice on co-ordinating with other trades (plumber, electrician)

faqs.q: How long does a kitchen survey take?
faqs.a: Most kitchen surveys take 45 to 90 minutes depending on the size and complexity of the kitchen.

faqs.q: Is a kitchen survey free?
faqs.a: Some fitters offer free surveys; others charge a survey fee that is credited back against the installation cost. At Install My Kitchen, the survey fee is £195, credited back on booking.

faqs.q: Do I need to have my kitchen plans ready for the survey?
faqs.a: Yes. The surveyor needs to see your retailer's design drawings to check room dimensions against the plan. Bring any correspondence from your kitchen designer too.

title: How Much Does Kitchen Removal Cost?
metaDescription: Find out how much it costs to remove an old kitchen before a new installation. Typical prices and what affects the cost.
answer: Kitchen removal typically costs between £200 and £600 depending on the size of the kitchen and whether disposal is included. Some kitchen fitters include removal as part of their installation service; others charge separately.
bodyHtml:
What Kitchen Removal Involves

Kitchen removal includes stripping out all existing units, worktops, plinths and cornices, disconnecting the sink, and making good where needed before the new kitchen installation begins. It does not typically include gas disconnection (which requires a Gas Safe registered engineer) or electrical isolation beyond standard safety procedures.

What Affects the Cost

  • The number of units being removed
  • Whether disposal to a tip is included or arranged separately
  • Whether any services need isolating before removal begins
  • The accessibility of the property for removing bulky waste

Disposal Options

Some kitchen fitters will take the old kitchen away as part of their removal service. Alternatively, you can arrange a skip or book a bulky waste collection with your local council. Removing and disposing of the old kitchen yourself before the fitter arrives can also reduce your total cost.

faqs.q: Do kitchen fitters remove old kitchens?
faqs.a: Many do, but not all include it as standard. Confirm whether kitchen removal and disposal is included in your quote.

faqs.q: Can I remove my old kitchen myself?
faqs.a: Yes, if you are confident and your services have been properly isolated by a qualified plumber and electrician. Removing the old kitchen yourself before the fitter arrives can save money.

faqs.q: Does kitchen removal take a full day?
faqs.a: For an average kitchen, removal typically takes half a day to one full day depending on size and the condition of existing fittings.

title: Should Flooring Go Before or After Kitchen Installation?
metaDescription: Find out the correct order: should you lay flooring before or after kitchen unit installation? The answer depends on the type of flooring and kitchen.
answer: Generally, rigid flooring (tiles, engineered wood, luxury vinyl tile) goes in before kitchen units, while soft flooring (carpet) goes in after. However, the specific answer depends on your kitchen type and flooring material.
bodyHtml:
The General Rule

Kitchen units are typically installed on top of the finished floor (or a subfloor at the same level). This means rigid flooring is usually laid before unit installation. The alternative — laying flooring after kitchen installation — makes it harder to achieve a clean finish around plinths and can cause problems if units ever need to be replaced.

Exceptions and Considerations

  • Underfloor heating systems need to be installed and commissioned before units go in
  • If you are fitting directly onto the subfloor and adding a floating floor layer later, your kitchen fitter needs to account for the floor height in their levelling
  • Tiled floors in particular benefit from being fully laid and grouted before units arrive

Co-ordinating Your Trades

Discuss the flooring sequence with your kitchen fitter at survey stage. A good fitter will tell you exactly what floor state they need to work on and how much height to allow for any floor coverings being added later.

faqs.q: Can flooring go under kitchen units?
faqs.a: It can, but it is not always necessary. Some fitters prefer the floor to run to the unit line only, with plinths covering the transition. Discuss this with your fitter.

faqs.q: What flooring is best under a kitchen?
faqs.a: Rigid flooring options — porcelain tile, engineered wood or LVT — are most practical under kitchen units. Ensure whatever you use is level and suitable for the weight of the kitchen.

faqs.q: Does flooring affect kitchen unit levelling?
faqs.a: Yes. An uneven floor requires more levelling adjustment on the plinth legs. Your kitchen fitter needs to know about any floor height changes before starting installation.

title: What Is the Difference Between Rigid and Flatpack Kitchens?
metaDescription: Rigid kitchens arrive pre-assembled; flatpack kitchens are assembled on site. Find out the differences, pros, cons and how it affects your installation.
answer: Rigid kitchens arrive with carcasses pre-assembled; flatpack kitchens arrive in flat panels that are assembled on site. Rigid kitchens typically install faster; flatpack gives more flexibility in tight access situations.
bodyHtml:
Rigid Kitchens

Rigid (or pre-assembled) kitchen units arrive with the carcass already built. Brands like Wren, Howdens and Magnet supply rigid units. Installation is typically faster as the fitter does not need to assemble the box before fixing it. Rigid units are generally considered more structurally stable in the long term.

Flatpack Kitchens

Flatpack kitchens — most notably IKEA — arrive as flat panels that are assembled by the kitchen fitter. This takes longer but can be advantageous where access is limited (narrow doorways, tight staircases) as each panel is smaller and easier to move. The finished result, when assembled correctly, is structurally sound.

Which Is Better?

  • Rigid units are faster to install and are the standard in the UK kitchen market
  • Flatpack units may be preferable where access is very restricted
  • Both types, when installed by a skilled fitter, produce excellent results

faqs.q: Are rigid kitchens more expensive to install?
faqs.a: Not necessarily. While rigid kitchens can install faster, fitter rates are typically based on unit count and complexity rather than rigid vs flatpack.

faqs.q: Is IKEA always flatpack?
faqs.a: Yes, IKEA's METOD and SEKTION ranges are flatpack systems assembled on site. Some other brands offer a mix of rigid and flatpack options.

faqs.q: Does a rigid kitchen last longer than flatpack?
faqs.a: Rigid units tend to be more robust structurally, but a well-assembled flatpack from a reputable brand like IKEA can last as long with proper care.

title: Can You Fit a Kitchen Without a Survey?
metaDescription: Is it possible to fit a kitchen without a pre-installation survey? Find out why a survey matters and the risks of skipping it.
answer: Technically yes, but it is strongly inadvisable. A pre-installation survey identifies discrepancies between plans and reality, structural issues and service positions before work begins. Skipping it often leads to delays, additional costs and a poorer finish.
bodyHtml:
What a Survey Prevents

The most common kitchen installation problems — units that don't fit, worktops cut to the wrong dimensions, services in unexpected positions — are all problems that a thorough survey would have identified in advance. Without a survey, these problems surface on installation day, causing delays and additional costs.

What Can Go Wrong Without a Survey

  • Units may not fit if room dimensions differ from the designer's plan
  • Appliances may not reach service connections in the positions planned
  • Floors that are more uneven than anticipated can require significant remedial work
  • Chimney breasts, alcoves or boxed-in pipework that weren't accounted for in the plan

Why Survey-First Is Standard Practice

Professional kitchen fitters survey before every job. It is the only way to give an accurate fixed price and to ensure installation proceeds without preventable surprises. Any fitter who quotes without visiting the room is either guessing or building contingency into their price.

faqs.q: How much does a kitchen survey cost?
faqs.a: Kitchen survey costs range from free (charged into the installation price) to around £200 as a standalone fee, typically credited back on booking.

faqs.q: Can I do my own measurements for a kitchen fitter?
faqs.a: You can provide measurements to help a fitter prepare, but a professional survey requires the fitter to assess the room in person — measurements alone are not a substitute.

faqs.q: What if the kitchen retailer has already done a survey?
faqs.a: Retailer surveys are design surveys, not installation surveys. They confirm the kitchen can be sold and designed; they do not assess installation conditions in the same depth as a specialist pre-installation survey.

title: How Do You Fit a Kitchen Around a Chimney Breast?
metaDescription: Fitting a kitchen around a chimney breast requires careful planning, scribing and custom solutions. Find out how professional fitters handle this challenge.
answer: Fitting around a chimney breast requires accurate measurement of the breast's position and projection, careful planning of unit placement, scribing of panels to the breast's surface, and sometimes the use of filler pieces or floating shelves to make the most of the alcove spaces.
bodyHtml:
The Main Challenges

A chimney breast projects into the kitchen space, interrupting a straight run of units. The breast is rarely perfectly plumb or square, which means panels that sit against it need to be scribed (cut to the exact profile of the surface) for a clean, gap-free finish.

Common Solutions

  • Alcove units or open shelving fitted into the spaces either side of the breast
  • The chimney breast used as a feature — for a range cooker, for example
  • Infill panels scribed to the breast face to close any gaps between adjacent units
  • Custom-width units ordered to maximise the usable space on either side

Survey Essentials

A pre-installation survey must include precise measurement of the chimney breast — its width, depth of projection and the degree to which it is out of plumb. This data drives the kitchen design and the installation plan. Without it, units will not fit cleanly.

faqs.q: Can units be placed in front of a chimney breast?
faqs.a: Yes, but the projection means units may need to be set forward of the main run, requiring careful adjustment of worktop depths and wall unit positions.

faqs.q: What is scribing in kitchen installation?
faqs.a: Scribing is the process of cutting a panel or unit to exactly match the profile of an adjacent surface — a wall, chimney breast or alcove — so no gap is visible.

faqs.q: Do chimney breasts make kitchen fitting more expensive?
faqs.a: Yes, typically. The additional measurement, planning and scribing work adds time and therefore cost to the installation.

title: What Is a Kitchen Fitter Day Rate?
metaDescription: What is a typical kitchen fitter day rate in the UK? Find out current rates, what affects them, and why day rates differ from fixed-price quotes.
answer: Kitchen fitter day rates in the UK typically range from £180 to £350 per day depending on experience, location and whether a team of two is involved. Day rates are distinct from fixed-price quotes, which are calculated based on the total scope of the job.
bodyHtml:
Day Rate vs Fixed Price

A day rate is an open-ended cost — you pay for however many days the installation takes. A fixed-price quote gives you a total cost regardless of how long the job takes. Most professional kitchen fitters prefer to quote fixed prices because they are clearer and fairer for the customer.

What Affects a Kitchen Fitter's Day Rate

  • Experience and skill level
  • Geographic location (rates are higher in London and the South East)
  • Whether one fitter or a team of two is working
  • The complexity of the kitchen and any specialist requirements

Why Fixed Quotes Are Usually Better

A fixed-price quote removes the risk of cost overruns from your side. If the fitter encounters additional challenges, they absorb the time cost rather than passing it to you. Fixed pricing requires a pre-installation survey to calculate accurately.

faqs.q: How many days does kitchen fitting take?
faqs.a: A small kitchen (8–12 units) takes 3–4 days; a medium kitchen (13–20 units) takes 4–6 days; a large kitchen (21+ units) takes 6–10 days.

faqs.q: Are kitchen fitter day rates negotiable?
faqs.a: Day rates have limited flexibility. Fixed-price quotes can sometimes be adjusted based on scope, but the best way to get value is to compare multiple fixed-price quotes after surveys.

faqs.q: Should I pay a kitchen fitter by the day or fixed price?
faqs.a: Fixed price is generally preferable — it removes uncertainty about final cost and encourages the fitter to work efficiently.

title: Can I Negotiate Kitchen Installation Price?
metaDescription: Can you negotiate kitchen installation costs? Find out what is flexible, what isn't, and how to get the best value from your kitchen fitter.
answer: Some negotiation is possible, particularly on scope or timing, but the best approach is to get multiple survey-based fixed quotes and compare them. A professional fitter's fixed price reflects actual labour costs and should not be heavily discounted.
bodyHtml:
What You Can Adjust

If a quote is higher than expected, there are legitimate ways to reduce it. Removing a particular scope item (such as appliance installation or removal of the old kitchen), adjusting the installation timeline to suit the fitter's schedule, or reducing the number of units can all bring the price down.

What You Should Not Do

Pressing a fitter to simply cut their price without a change in scope is counterproductive. A fitter who drops their price without explanation may be planning to cut corners, rush the job or use lower-quality materials. The right question is always 'what can we adjust in scope to reduce the price?' not 'can you do it cheaper?'

The Best Way to Get Value

  • Get at least two or three fixed-price quotes based on actual surveys
  • Compare what each quote includes — the cheapest quote may include less
  • Prioritise experience and accountability over headline price

faqs.q: Is the cheapest kitchen fitter the best value?
faqs.a: Not always. The cheapest quote may exclude items that others include, or reflect a less experienced fitter. Total value includes quality, accountability and a clean, complete finish.

faqs.q: Can I save money by supplying my own materials?
faqs.a: In some cases, yes — for example, supplying your own worktop material. Discuss this with your fitter at survey stage.

faqs.q: Do kitchen fitters charge VAT?
faqs.a: It depends on whether the fitter is VAT registered. Always clarify whether a quote is inclusive or exclusive of VAT before accepting.

title: How Long Does It Take to Fit an IKEA Kitchen?
metaDescription: Find out how long it takes to professionally fit an IKEA kitchen. Typical timescales by kitchen size and what affects installation time.
answer: An IKEA kitchen typically takes 4–7 days to fit professionally, depending on the number of units and complexity. Flatpack assembly adds time compared to rigid kitchens, but an experienced IKEA fitter will work efficiently.
bodyHtml:
IKEA vs Rigid Kitchen Installation Time

Because IKEA kitchens are flatpack, the fitter must assemble each carcass on site before fixing it in position. This adds approximately 20–30% to the time needed compared with a rigid kitchen of the same unit count.

Typical IKEA Installation Timescales

  • Small kitchen (10–14 units): 4–5 days
  • Medium kitchen (15–22 units): 5–7 days
  • Large kitchen (23+ units): 7–10 days

What Adds Time

Corner unit carousels, integrated appliances (particularly tall housing units), island units and unusual wall configurations all add to installation time. A pre-installation survey will identify these factors in advance and allow the fitter to plan accurately.

faqs.q: Can IKEA kitchen fitting be done in a day?
faqs.a: Not for a typical kitchen. IKEA fitting involves flatpack assembly as well as fixing, levelling, worktop fitting and finishing — this requires multiple days.

faqs.q: Do I need to assemble IKEA units before the fitter arrives?
faqs.a: No. A professional kitchen fitter will assemble and install all units. Do not pre-assemble carcasses as the fitter may need to adapt assembly during installation.

faqs.q: Does IKEA kitchen fitting include the worktop?
faqs.a: Yes, if worktop fitting is included in your quote. Confirm this at survey stage. IKEA worktops require cutting and edging on site.

title: What Is Kitchen Second Fix?
metaDescription: Kitchen second fix refers to the second stage of plumbing and electrical work done after units and worktops are fitted. Find out what it involves.
answer: Kitchen second fix is the second phase of plumbing and electrical work, carried out after the kitchen units and worktops are installed. It includes final connection of the sink, tap, dishwasher and appliances to services that were roughed in during first fix.
bodyHtml:
First Fix vs Second Fix

First fix plumbing and electrical work happens before the kitchen is installed — running pipes and cables to the approximate positions of future appliances and fittings. Second fix happens after the kitchen is in: connecting those pipes and cables to the actual sink, appliances and outlets.

What Second Fix Plumbing Covers

  • Connecting the sink waste and supply
  • Fitting and commissioning the tap
  • Connecting the dishwasher supply and waste
  • Final connection of any other water-fed appliances

What Second Fix Electrical Covers

  • Fitting sockets in kitchen positions
  • Connecting integrated appliances to circuits
  • Installing under-cabinet lighting
  • Final connection of the cooker/hob circuit

faqs.q: Who does kitchen second fix?
faqs.a: Second fix plumbing is done by a qualified plumber; second fix electrical work must be done by a qualified electrician. Some kitchen fitters have plumbing qualifications and can do basic second fix plumbing.

faqs.q: When does second fix happen in a kitchen installation?
faqs.a: Second fix happens after units and worktops are installed and in position. It is one of the final stages of the kitchen installation process.

faqs.q: Do kitchen fitters co-ordinate with plumbers and electricians?
faqs.a: Usually yes — your kitchen fitter will advise on the correct sequence and timing for second fix trades. It is your responsibility to book them, but a good fitter will communicate the schedule clearly.

title: How Do You Hang Kitchen Wall Units?
metaDescription: Find out how kitchen wall units are hung, what fixings are used and how fitters ensure they are level and secure.
answer: Kitchen wall units are hung using a wall rail system or direct-fix brackets into wall studs or solid masonry. The key steps are marking the correct height, locating fixings points, hanging the rail or brackets level, and attaching and adjusting the unit suspension brackets.
bodyHtml:
Wall Rail Systems

Most modern kitchen unit systems use a metal wall rail (or hanging rail) that is fixed to the wall at a precise height. Units are then hooked onto this rail via adjustable brackets, allowing them to be levelled and adjusted without re-drilling. The rail spreads the load across multiple fixing points.

Direct-Fix Methods

Some kitchen systems, particularly older designs, use direct screw fixing into the back of the unit carcass through the wall panel. This requires accurate location of studs or use of appropriate wall anchors for masonry.

Key Considerations

  • Wall construction must be established before choosing fixings — timber stud, metal stud and masonry all require different approaches
  • The wall unit height must be set in relation to both ceiling height and base unit worktop height
  • Levelling is adjusted via the bracket mechanism after hanging

faqs.q: How high should kitchen wall units be hung?
faqs.a: Standard wall unit height positions the base of the wall unit approximately 450mm above the worktop surface, though this varies by ceiling height and preference.

faqs.q: Can wall units be hung on a plasterboard wall?
faqs.a: Yes, but fixings must be located in studs or use specialist hollow wall anchors rated for the load. A professional kitchen fitter will assess wall construction and choose the correct fixings.

faqs.q: What if my wall is not straight behind the wall units?
faqs.a: Wall units can be packed out at the fixing points to accommodate a slightly uneven wall. Significant undulations may require battening before units are hung.

title: What Happens If Kitchen Units Don't Fit?
metaDescription: What happens when kitchen units don't fit in the space? Find out how fitters handle this problem and how to avoid it with a proper survey.
answer: If kitchen units don't fit, the installation must pause while solutions are found. This might involve ordering a different-width unit, using filler panels, adjusting the layout, or in serious cases, returning to the retailer. A thorough pre-installation survey prevents most fitting problems.
bodyHtml:
Why Units Sometimes Don't Fit

The most common reason is a discrepancy between the kitchen designer's plan dimensions and the actual room dimensions. Walls that are not plumb, floors that are not level, or alcoves that are smaller than specified on the plan all cause units to not fit as designed.

What the Fitter Can Do

  • Use filler panels (infill strips) to close gaps between units and walls
  • Order a modified-width unit from the retailer if a significant gap exists
  • Adjust the layout within the run to redistribute space
  • In some cases, trim a carcass (though this is not always possible depending on material)

Why Surveys Prevent This

A professional pre-installation survey cross-checks every dimension on the plan against the actual room. Problems are identified before the kitchen is delivered, allowing corrections to the order before installation begins — not during it.

faqs.q: Who is responsible if kitchen units don't fit?
faqs.a: If the room dimensions were correctly communicated and the retailer designed around them, responsibility lies with the retailer. If the room was mismeasured, responsibility depends on who measured it.

faqs.q: Can filler panels always fix a sizing problem?
faqs.a: Filler panels work well for small gaps (typically up to 100mm on each side). Larger discrepancies require design amendments or new units.

faqs.q: How quickly can replacement units be ordered?
faqs.a: Lead times vary by retailer. Some can supply individual units within 3–5 days; others take 2–4 weeks. This is why catching problems at survey stage is so important.

title: Do Kitchen Fitters Work in the Rain?
metaDescription: Kitchen installation is an indoor trade. Find out how weather affects kitchen fitting and what to expect on installation days.
answer: Yes. Kitchen installation is an indoor trade and is not typically affected by rain. Fitters will work regardless of weather conditions outdoors. External factors like deliveries or access may occasionally be affected, but the fitting work itself is not.
bodyHtml:
Indoor Work

Kitchen fitting happens entirely inside the property. Rain, wind and cold weather outdoors have no impact on the installation process. A professional kitchen fitter will arrive on time and work through their full scheduled day regardless of the weather.

What Can Be Affected by Weather

  • Deliveries — if access roads are affected or delivery vehicles have delays
  • Worktop template visits — no impact
  • Worktop deliveries for heavy stone slabs may be postponed in extreme conditions

Practical Considerations

If your installation requires work near external openings (such as a new extractor fan duct or an external waste run), the fitter will work around weather conditions for those specific tasks but will continue with other elements of the installation in the meantime.

faqs.q: Will bad weather delay my kitchen installation?
faqs.a: Rarely. Kitchen fitting is indoor work and weather has minimal impact. Delivery delays are the most likely weather-related issue.

faqs.q: What happens if a delivery is delayed due to weather?
faqs.a: Your fitter may continue with other elements of the installation while waiting for delivery, or reschedule the affected day. This is agreed between you and the fitter.

faqs.q: Do kitchen fitters work in winter?
faqs.a: Yes. Kitchen installation continues year-round. Winter scheduling is usually straightforward as there is no weather dependency for the work itself.

title: What Is a Kitchen Capping Piece?
metaDescription: Find out what a kitchen capping piece is, where it is used and how it contributes to a professional finished look.
answer: A kitchen capping piece is a decorative strip fitted to the top of a tall unit or wall unit to cover the gap between the unit and the ceiling. It provides a neat finish and is usually colour-matched to the kitchen doors or carcass.
bodyHtml:
Where Capping Pieces Are Used

Capping pieces are typically used above tall larder units, fridge housing units or wall units where the unit does not reach the ceiling. Rather than leaving a visible gap, a capping piece (sometimes called a capping panel or light pelmet in different contexts) provides a continuous, finished appearance.

Types of Capping Piece

  • Flat capping strips that sit on top of a wall unit rail
  • Decorative capping that matches the door profile
  • Pelmet pieces that incorporate lighting channels for under-cabinet LED strips

How They Are Fitted

Capping pieces are usually cut to length on site and fixed to the top of the unit or wall with small fixings or adhesive. Where the kitchen has a cornice, the capping piece may be integrated with or replaced by the cornice moulding.

faqs.q: Is a capping piece the same as a cornice?
faqs.a: No, though they serve a similar purpose. A cornice is a decorative moulding applied to the top front of wall units; a capping piece is a flat panel fitted to the top of a unit.

faqs.q: Do all kitchens need capping pieces?
faqs.a: Not all. Floor-to-ceiling units eliminate the need for capping. In kitchens with standard-height wall units, capping pieces or cornices are typically used to finish the top of the run.

faqs.q: Are capping pieces supplied by the kitchen retailer?
faqs.a: Usually yes — they are part of the kitchen specification. If they are not in your order, ask your retailer before installation begins.

title: Should You Tile Before or After Kitchen Units?
metaDescription: When should kitchen wall tiles go in — before or after units are fitted? The correct sequence explained.
answer: Wall tiles in the kitchen splashback area go in after units and worktops are installed. The worktop edge sets the bottom tile line. Tiling before units and worktops makes it impossible to set the correct tile height and risks damage to tiles during installation.
bodyHtml:
The Correct Sequence

Kitchen wall tiling follows this order: (1) units installed and levelled; (2) worktops templated (if stone) and fitted; (3) tiler marks the tile line from the worktop surface and completes the splashback. This ensures tiles sit correctly relative to the worktop and look professional.

Why You Don't Tile First

Tiling before units means the tiler is working to theoretical dimensions that may not match the installed worktop height exactly. Variations in floor level, unit levelling and worktop thickness all affect the final worktop surface height — and therefore where the bottom tile line should sit.

Exceptions

  • Feature tiles or large-format tiles that run behind units may need to be fitted before installation to avoid visible breaks
  • If tiling the full wall (not just the splashback), discuss the sequence with your kitchen fitter and tiler before either trade starts work

faqs.q: Can the kitchen fitter and tiler work at the same time?
faqs.a: Usually not on the same area. It is better to sequence these trades: units first, worktop fitting, then tiling. Overlapping trades in the same space causes inefficiency and potential damage.

faqs.q: Do kitchen fitters tile as well?
faqs.a: Some kitchen fitters offer tiling as part of their service. Many do not. Confirm at survey stage whether tiling is included in your quote or whether you need to arrange a separate tiler.

faqs.q: What is the gap between worktop and wall tiles?
faqs.a: Typically 0–2mm, sealed with a silicone bead. The tiler sets the bottom tile level to sit neatly on the worktop surface, with the silicone joint hidden below.

title: How Much Does a Medium Kitchen Cost to Install?
metaDescription: What is the typical installation cost for a medium-sized kitchen? Find out current labour rates and what affects the price.
answer: A medium kitchen of 13–20 units typically costs £1,800 to £3,200 to install in the UK, depending on complexity, kitchen type and location. This is for labour only and excludes kitchen units, worktops and appliances.
bodyHtml:
What Is a Medium Kitchen?

A medium kitchen is typically defined as one with 13–20 units (base and wall combined). This usually covers a kitchen of around 12–18 square metres or an L-shaped or galley kitchen in a 3–4 bedroom property.

What Affects the Installation Cost

  • Number of units and their complexity (corner units, tall housing, island)
  • Worktop type — laminate is cut on site; stone worktops require a separate template and fitting visit
  • Appliance integration — integrated appliances take longer to fit than freestanding
  • Old kitchen removal — typically adds £200–£400 to the total

How to Get an Accurate Price

The only reliable way to get an accurate installation price is to have a professional pre-installation survey. A surveyor will assess your kitchen plan, the room conditions and the full scope of work before providing a fixed quote.

faqs.q: Does a medium kitchen installation include worktop fitting?
faqs.a: Usually yes, for a laminate or wood worktop cut on site. Stone worktops require a separate template visit and fitting appointment, which may be quoted separately.

faqs.q: How long does a medium kitchen take to fit?
faqs.a: A medium kitchen of 13–20 units typically takes 4–6 days for a professional kitchen fitter.

faqs.q: Is VAT included in kitchen installation quotes?
faqs.a: Not always — check whether your quote is inclusive or exclusive of VAT, particularly if the fitter is not VAT registered.

title: Can a Kitchen Fitter Also Do the Electrics?
metaDescription: Can a kitchen fitter carry out electrical work in your kitchen? Find out what qualifications are needed and when you need a separate electrician.
answer: Some kitchen fitters hold electrical qualifications (such as Part P or ECS) and can carry out certain kitchen electrical work. Most do not. For notifiable electrical work — including new circuits, consumer unit changes and cooker connections — a qualified electrician is required.
bodyHtml:
What Electrical Work a Kitchen Fitter Might Do

A kitchen fitter without electrical qualifications can legitimately carry out like-for-like socket replacements and some minor electrical work that is not notifiable under Part P. This includes swapping a like-for-like socket in the same position or connecting a plug-in appliance.

What Requires a Qualified Electrician

  • New circuits (additional sockets, dedicated appliance circuits)
  • Moving or adding consumer unit circuits
  • Cooker/hob connections (these require a dedicated circuit)
  • Any work in a kitchen that needs a Building Regulations notification

The Practical Approach

Book a qualified electrician for first fix (before units are installed) and second fix (after units and worktops are in place). Coordinate with your kitchen fitter so the electrician arrives at the right stages. Your kitchen fitter should advise on the sequence.

faqs.q: Does a kitchen fitter need to be an electrician?
faqs.a: No. Kitchen installation is a separate trade from electrical work. Most kitchen fitters focus on cabinetry, worktops and appliance fitting, and co-ordinate with a separate electrician for electrical work.

faqs.q: What is Part P and does it apply to kitchens?
faqs.a: Part P is the Building Regulations requirement for electrical installation work in dwellings. Most new kitchen electrical work is notifiable under Part P and must be carried out or certified by a competent person.

faqs.q: Can I book my own electrician separately from the kitchen fitter?
faqs.a: Yes. You are responsible for booking and co-ordinating the electrician. A good kitchen fitter will advise on timing and communicate the installation schedule so trades arrive at the right stages.

title: What Does It Mean When a Kitchen Is on Back Order?
metaDescription: What does 'back order' mean for kitchen units? Find out how back orders affect your installation schedule and what to do.
answer: A kitchen unit or component on back order means it is temporarily out of stock at the supplier and will be dispatched when stock is replenished. It can delay your installation if critical items are unavailable on delivery day.
bodyHtml:
Why Back Orders Happen

Kitchen retailers hold stock of popular lines but may not have every unit in every colour and size available immediately. Supply chain issues, high demand, or delays from manufacturers can result in individual items being on back order at the point of delivery.

How Back Orders Affect Installation

If a back-ordered item is a critical unit (a corner unit, a tall housing or a key base cabinet), installation may need to be paused or rescheduled. Non-critical items (such as a single door or a plinth strip) can sometimes be managed by continuing installation and fitting the missing item when it arrives.

What to Do If You Have Back-Ordered Items

  • Confirm with your retailer exactly which items are on back order and the expected delivery date
  • Discuss with your kitchen fitter whether installation can proceed with the missing items
  • Do not schedule installation until all critical items are confirmed delivered and checked

faqs.q: Can I start kitchen installation with items missing?
faqs.a: Sometimes, yes — if non-critical items are missing. Your fitter will advise on what is needed before work can begin and what can be fitted retrospectively.

faqs.q: Who is responsible for back-ordered items?
faqs.a: The kitchen retailer is responsible for fulfilling the complete order. If back orders affect your installation date, you may be able to claim compensation from the retailer for any resulting costs.

faqs.q: How long do kitchen back orders typically take?
faqs.a: Back order lead times vary — from a few days to several weeks depending on the supplier and item. Always get a confirmed delivery date in writing.

title: What Is the Difference Between a Kitchen Installer and a Builder?
metaDescription: Kitchen installers and builders both work on kitchens, but their roles are different. Find out who does what and which trade you need.
answer: A kitchen installer is a specialist in fitting kitchen units, worktops and appliances. A builder handles structural work, extensions and general construction. You may need both for a full kitchen renovation but each does a distinct job.
bodyHtml:
What a Kitchen Installer Does

A specialist kitchen installer focuses on the assembly and fitting of kitchen units, doors, worktops, plinths, cornices and integrated appliances. They work to a high standard of precision — door alignment, worktop joints, levelling on uneven floors — that a general builder typically does not specialise in.

What a Builder Does

A builder handles structural work such as extending a room, moving walls, installing steels, laying new floor structures or building in a new fireplace. If your kitchen project involves any structural changes to the space, a builder is needed before a kitchen installer begins their work.

The Right Sequence

  • Structural work (builder) → first fix plumbing and electrics (plumber/electrician) → kitchen installation (kitchen installer) → second fix plumbing and electrics → tiling (tiler)

faqs.q: Can a builder fit a kitchen?
faqs.a: Some builders have kitchen fitting experience, but a specialist kitchen installer will typically achieve a better finish, particularly on door alignment, gap consistency and worktop jointing.

faqs.q: Do I need a builder before a kitchen installation?
faqs.a: Only if structural work is required — knocking walls, installing steels, adding extensions. For a like-for-like kitchen replacement, a builder is usually not needed.

faqs.q: Who manages the different trades on a kitchen project?
faqs.a: Usually the homeowner co-ordinates the trades unless a project manager or main contractor is appointed. Your kitchen installer can advise on the correct sequence of works.

title: How Do You Stop Kitchen Units Creaking?
metaDescription: Kitchen units that creak or squeak after installation can be fixed. Find out the causes and how a professional kitchen fitter addresses this.
answer: Creaking kitchen units are usually caused by units that are not fully secured, movement in a wooden subfloor, or hinge mechanisms that need adjustment. A professional kitchen fitter will identify and fix the cause during the snagging process.
bodyHtml:
Common Causes of Creaking

  • Base units not firmly fixed to the wall or floor, allowing movement under load
  • Hinge adjusters that have worked loose
  • Wooden subfloor movement transferring to kitchen units above
  • Plinth legs that are not fully tensioned or bedded on the floor

How Creaking Is Fixed

A kitchen fitter will re-examine all fixing points on the affected units, re-tighten or re-fix any that have moved, and adjust or replace hinge mechanisms as needed. Creaking from floor movement may require additional plinth leg support or the introduction of anti-creak pads.

Preventing Creaking at Installation

The best prevention is a thorough installation in the first place — properly fixed units, correctly adjusted hinges, and plinths that sit flat and firm. A quality kitchen installation should not creak.

faqs.q: Is creaking covered by a kitchen installation guarantee?
faqs.a: Creaking that is a result of poor installation should be covered by your fitter's guarantee. Document the problem and contact your fitter promptly.

faqs.q: Can I fix creaking kitchen units myself?
faqs.a: Minor hinge adjustment or tightening of visible fixings can be done by a confident homeowner. For more significant movement, it is better to have your fitter investigate.

faqs.q: How long does it take for a kitchen fitter to fix a creak?
faqs.a: Most creak issues can be identified and resolved in under an hour during a snagging visit. Inform your fitter as soon as you notice the problem.

title: What Is a Kitchen Larder Unit?
metaDescription: Find out what a kitchen larder unit is, how it is used and what to consider when having one installed.
answer: A kitchen larder unit is a tall, full-height storage cabinet with multiple shelves or internal fittings, used for storing dry goods, food, or kitchen equipment. It offers maximum storage in a single floor-to-ceiling column.
bodyHtml:
Types of Larder Unit

Larder units come in several configurations. A standard larder has fixed or adjustable shelves throughout. A pull-out larder (also called a pantry unit) has internal frames that pull out for easy access to everything stored at the back. A larder housing can also be configured to hold a tall fridge-freezer with storage above or below.

Installation Considerations

  • Larder units are tall and heavy — site access and ceiling height must be assessed before ordering
  • Pull-out internal fittings must be installed in the correct sequence during assembly
  • If the unit is flanked by wall units, the wall units must be set back to allow the larder door to open fully

Where Larder Units Work Best

Larder units are particularly effective in galley kitchens where wall space is premium, in open-plan kitchens where a dedicated storage column keeps work surfaces clear, and in family kitchens where food storage capacity is a priority.

faqs.q: Can I have a larder unit in a small kitchen?
faqs.a: Yes. A single tall larder unit can provide more storage than several wall units and may make better use of limited floor space.

faqs.q: Is a larder unit the same as a pantry?
faqs.a: Not exactly. A pantry can refer to a dedicated room or large storage space; a larder unit is a single tall cabinet. The terms are sometimes used interchangeably for tall kitchen storage.

faqs.q: Does a larder unit need specialist installation?
faqs.a: Not specialist, but it does benefit from experienced installation — particularly for units with pull-out internal fittings, which must be assembled in the correct order during installation.

title: How Much Does It Cost to Refit a Small Kitchen?
metaDescription: What does it cost to refit a small kitchen? Find out the typical costs for labour, units and other elements of a small kitchen refurbishment.
answer: Refitting a small kitchen (8–12 units) typically costs £3,500–£8,000 in total, including new units, basic worktops and installation labour. Installation labour alone for a small kitchen is usually £1,200–£1,800.
bodyHtml:
What Is a Small Kitchen?

A small kitchen typically has 8–12 units and serves a 1–2 bedroom property or a compact kitchen space in a larger home. The installation itself takes 3–4 days for a professional kitchen fitter.

Cost Breakdown

  • Kitchen units (entry-level, e.g. Wickes or B&Q): £1,500–£3,000
  • Kitchen units (mid-range, e.g. Howdens or Magnet): £2,500–£5,000
  • Laminate worktop: £200–£500
  • Stone worktop: £800–£1,800
  • Installation labour: £1,200–£1,800
  • Old kitchen removal and disposal: £200–£400

Ways to Reduce Cost

Keeping the existing layout reduces plumbing and electrical relocation costs. Choosing a laminate worktop over stone is one of the biggest savings. Removing the old kitchen yourself (with services properly isolated) also reduces total cost.

faqs.q: How long does a small kitchen refit take?
faqs.a: A small kitchen refit typically takes 1–2 weeks from removal of the old kitchen to full completion, including any plumbing, electrical and tiling work.

faqs.q: Can I refit a small kitchen on a budget?
faqs.a: Yes. Using entry-level units from a high-street retailer, a laminate worktop and a simple layout keeps costs manageable without compromising on a professional finish.

faqs.q: Do I need a new kitchen or can I just refit?
faqs.a: If your existing unit carcasses are in good condition, a refresh (new doors, worktops and handles) can transform the kitchen at a fraction of the cost of a full refit.

title: Can You Move a Kitchen to a Different Room?
metaDescription: Is it possible to move a kitchen to a different room? Find out the structural, plumbing and electrical implications of relocating a kitchen.
answer: Yes, it is possible to move a kitchen to a different room, but it involves significant structural, plumbing and electrical work. This is a major project requiring multiple trades and planning — the kitchen itself is just one element.
bodyHtml:
What Moving a Kitchen Involves

Relocating a kitchen means extending or moving the existing plumbing supplies and waste runs to the new location, routing new electrical circuits, potentially modifying the structure (if walls are removed or new openings created), and fully stripping out the old kitchen location before installing in the new space.

Planning Considerations

  • Building regulations may apply if walls are being removed or structural changes made
  • Gas supply relocation requires a Gas Safe registered engineer
  • New drainage runs must be planned to achieve a suitable fall to the existing drain
  • The old kitchen location will need making good — plastering, electrics, flooring

The Right Team

A kitchen relocation requires a builder (for structural work), a plumber, a Gas Safe engineer (if gas is involved), an electrician and a kitchen installer. Coordinating all trades in the correct sequence is essential.

faqs.q: How much does it cost to move a kitchen to a different room?
faqs.a: Moving a kitchen to a different room typically costs £5,000–£15,000 or more, depending on the scale of structural, plumbing and electrical work required.

faqs.q: Does moving a kitchen require planning permission?
faqs.a: Not usually, but if the work involves structural changes, an extension or changes to the external appearance of the property, planning permission or building regulations approval may be required.

faqs.q: How long does it take to move a kitchen?
faqs.a: A kitchen relocation project typically takes 3–6 weeks from start to completion, depending on the complexity of the structural, plumbing and electrical work involved.

title: What Is the Difference Between an Integrated and Freestanding Kitchen?
metaDescription: Integrated kitchens have built-in appliances behind matching doors. Freestanding kitchens have standalone appliances. Find out the key differences.
answer: An integrated kitchen has appliances (fridge, dishwasher, oven, washing machine) built into the kitchen unit run, concealed behind matching cabinet doors. A freestanding kitchen uses standalone appliances that are not built into the cabinetry.
bodyHtml:
Integrated Kitchens

Integrated appliances are built into purpose-designed unit housings. The appliance door is either fixed to a matching kitchen door (door-on-door) or the door is integrated directly onto the appliance (fixed-door). The result is a seamless appearance where appliances are invisible within the kitchen run.

Freestanding Kitchens

Freestanding appliances stand independently and are not concealed within cabinetry. They are generally less expensive to purchase and replace, and offer more flexibility in kitchen layout. They are also easier to take with you if you move home.

Which Is Right for You?

  • Integrated kitchens offer a cleaner aesthetic and are preferred in modern, high-end kitchen designs
  • Freestanding appliances are more flexible, often cheaper and simpler to service and replace
  • Installation of integrated appliances is more complex and must be planned carefully at design stage

faqs.q: Does integrated kitchen installation cost more?
faqs.a: Yes. Integrated appliance fitting involves more complex housing assembly, door fixing and connection work than positioning freestanding appliances.

faqs.q: Can I convert a freestanding kitchen to integrated?
faqs.a: In some cases, yes — but it depends on whether the unit run has the correct housing units and whether the gap dimensions match the appliance. Discuss with your kitchen designer.

faqs.q: Do integrated appliances need more maintenance?
faqs.a: Not more maintenance, but accessing an integrated appliance for servicing can be more complex as the unit door and housing must be opened or removed.

title: Who Does the Gas Connection for a Kitchen?
metaDescription: Gas connections in a kitchen must be done by a Gas Safe registered engineer. Find out what gas work is involved in a kitchen installation and who does it.
answer: Gas connections in a kitchen must be carried out by a Gas Safe registered engineer. This includes connecting a gas cooker or hob, extending or relocating a gas supply, and any work on the gas supply infrastructure.
bodyHtml:
What Gas Work Is Needed in a Kitchen

Kitchen gas work typically involves connecting a gas hob or range cooker to the gas supply, and sometimes relocating the gas supply point if the cooker position is changing. Any work involving the gas pipework or connections must be done by a Gas Safe registered engineer.

Kitchen Fitters and Gas

Most kitchen fitters are not Gas Safe registered and cannot legally connect gas appliances. Your kitchen fitter will typically position the gas cooker or hob but leave the final gas connection to a Gas Safe engineer who visits after the unit installation is complete.

Booking a Gas Safe Engineer

  • Book your Gas Safe engineer before your installation date — they may need to visit twice (first fix and connection)
  • Ensure the engineer knows what appliance is being connected and where the supply point is
  • Request a certificate on completion of the gas connection

faqs.q: Can a kitchen fitter connect a gas hob?
faqs.a: Only if they are Gas Safe registered. Most kitchen fitters are not Gas Safe registered and cannot legally connect gas appliances.

faqs.q: How much does a Gas Safe engineer charge for a kitchen connection?
faqs.a: Gas Safe engineers typically charge £80–£200 for connecting a gas hob or cooker, depending on complexity and whether the supply point needs moving.

faqs.q: Do I get a certificate for gas connection?
faqs.a: Yes. A Gas Safe registered engineer must provide a certificate on completion of the gas connection work. Keep this for your records and for any future property sale.

title: How Do You Fit an Under-Counter Fridge?
metaDescription: Under-counter fridges can be freestanding, integrated or semi-integrated. Find out how each type is fitted and what the installation involves.
answer: An under-counter fridge is fitted by positioning it in the correct unit housing, ensuring adequate ventilation clearance, levelling it on the adjustable feet, and connecting it to the electrical supply. Integrated models have a matching kitchen door fixed to the appliance door.
bodyHtml:
Types of Under-Counter Fridge Installation

Freestanding under-counter fridges are simply positioned under the worktop and plugged in. Semi-integrated fridges have a kitchen door covering the top portion with the control panel exposed at the bottom. Fully integrated fridges are entirely concealed behind a matching kitchen door on a door-on-door or fixed-door hinge mechanism.

Key Installation Steps

  • Check ventilation clearance requirements — fridges need airflow around the body to operate efficiently
  • Level the appliance using the adjustable feet so the door seal is even
  • For integrated models, fix the kitchen door to the appliance door using the hinge kit supplied
  • Connect to the electrical supply — under-counter fridges use a standard 13A socket

Common Issues

The most common installation problem with under-counter fridges is insufficient ventilation clearance, which causes the fridge to run hot and inefficiently. Always check the manufacturer's ventilation requirements and allow the specified clearances.

faqs.q: Does an integrated fridge need a special socket?
faqs.a: No — integrated fridges use a standard 13A socket positioned inside the unit housing. Your electrician should position the socket inside the housing during first fix.

faqs.q: Can I fit an under-counter fridge myself?
faqs.a: A freestanding under-counter fridge can be positioned by a homeowner. Integrated models with door-fixing mechanisms are more complex and are best handled by a kitchen fitter.

faqs.q: How much ventilation does an under-counter fridge need?
faqs.a: Most under-counter fridges require 25–50mm clearance at the back and sides. Check the specific requirements in your appliance manual.

title: What Is the Best Worktop for a Kitchen?
metaDescription: Find out the pros and cons of the most popular kitchen worktop materials and which is best for your needs.
answer: The best worktop depends on your budget, lifestyle and aesthetic preference. Quartz is the most popular premium choice for durability and low maintenance; laminate offers excellent value; solid wood adds warmth; granite is hard-wearing and unique.
bodyHtml:
Quartz Worktops

Engineered quartz (such as Silestone or Caesarstone) is extremely hard-wearing, non-porous and available in a wide range of colours and finishes. It does not require sealing and resists staining well. It is the most popular premium worktop choice in UK kitchens. Installation requires specialist templating and fitting — quartz cannot be cut on site.

Laminate Worktops

Laminate offers excellent value and has improved significantly in quality and appearance. It is cut on site, making it faster and cheaper to install. It is not as heat or scratch resistant as stone but is perfectly practical for everyday use.

Solid Wood Worktops

Solid wood adds warmth and character. It requires regular oiling to maintain and can be sanded and refinished if it shows wear over time. It is not ideal near the sink area due to moisture sensitivity.

Granite

Natural granite is extremely hard-wearing and each slab is unique. It requires periodic sealing and is installed in the same way as quartz — templating followed by a separate fitting visit.

faqs.q: How much does a quartz worktop cost?
faqs.a: Quartz worktops typically cost £300–£900 per linear metre installed, depending on the brand, thickness and edge profile chosen.

faqs.q: Is laminate worktop good quality?
faqs.a: Yes. Modern laminate worktops from reputable suppliers are practical, durable and good-looking. They offer the best cost-to-quality ratio for most kitchens.

faqs.q: Can kitchen worktops be replaced without replacing the units?
faqs.a: Yes. Worktops are fitted on top of the units and can be replaced independently. The units must be temporarily disconnected from the worktop and any upstand or splashback removed first.

title: Can I Replace a Kitchen Worktop Without Replacing Units?
metaDescription: Yes, kitchen worktops can be replaced without replacing the units. Find out how the process works and what it costs.
answer: Yes. Kitchen worktops can be replaced without changing the units. The old worktop is removed, the new one is measured, cut and fitted in its place. This is one of the most cost-effective ways to refresh a kitchen.
bodyHtml:
What the Process Involves

The sink is disconnected and lifted out. The old worktop is unfixed from the units (usually via clips or brackets underneath) and removed. Any upstand or splashback fixed to the wall at the worktop line is also removed. The new worktop is then measured, cut and fitted. The sink is refitted and reconnected.

Important Considerations

  • Stone worktops (quartz, granite) require a template visit before fabrication — the new slab cannot be cut until the units are confirmed in position
  • If the tiles or splashback behind the old worktop are different in height or colour from what was behind it, this may need re-tiling
  • The unit tops must be clean, level and sound for the new worktop to sit correctly

Cost Benefit

A new worktop can transform the look of a kitchen at a fraction of the cost of full replacement. If units are structurally sound and the layout works, worktop replacement alone can make a substantial visual difference.

faqs.q: How long does worktop replacement take?
faqs.a: Laminate worktop replacement can typically be completed in one day. Stone worktops require a template visit (day 1), fabrication (7–14 days), and a fitting day.

faqs.q: Does replacing a worktop require a plumber?
faqs.a: The sink connection and disconnection involves basic plumbing. Some kitchen fitters can handle this; others will advise you to book a plumber for the sink reconnection.

faqs.q: Can I keep my existing sink when replacing the worktop?
faqs.a: Usually yes, if the sink is in good condition and the new worktop can be cut for the same sink cut-out dimensions.

title: How Do Kitchen Fitters Deal With Uneven Walls?
metaDescription: Uneven walls are common in older UK properties. Find out how professional kitchen fitters handle this challenge.
answer: Kitchen fitters deal with uneven walls by scribing panels and units to the wall profile, using filler pieces to close gaps, or packing units out from the wall where necessary. The goal is a clean, gap-free result regardless of the wall condition.
bodyHtml:
Scribing

Scribing is the primary technique for dealing with uneven walls. A panel, end panel or filler piece is held against the wall and a scribe line is drawn on it using a compass or scribing tool. The panel is then cut to that line and sits perfectly against the wall's actual surface.

Packing Out

Where a wall has a significant bulge in the centre but is flatter at the sides, units may be packed out from the wall to clear the bulge. This creates a gap between the back of the unit and the wall, which is then covered by the end panel or by a filler strip.

When Walls Need More Work

If walls are severely out of plumb (more than 20–25mm variation), plastering or dot-and-dab plasterboard may be needed before kitchen installation to create a workable surface. A thorough pre-installation survey will identify if this level of preparation is required.

faqs.q: Are uneven walls common in UK kitchens?
faqs.a: Very common, particularly in Victorian, Edwardian and early post-war properties. Experienced kitchen fitters expect this and plan for it.

faqs.q: Will uneven walls affect the kitchen installation price?
faqs.a: Significantly uneven walls may add time to the installation and therefore cost. A good survey will identify this in advance so the quote accounts for it.

faqs.q: Does scribing take a long time?
faqs.a: Scribing a panel accurately takes 20–45 minutes. On a kitchen with multiple awkward wall junctions, scribing work can add half a day or more to the overall installation time.

title: What Is a Belfast Sink and How Is It Fitted?
metaDescription: Belfast sinks are heavy, traditional ceramic sinks that require special fitting. Find out how they are installed and what to consider.
answer: A Belfast sink is a large, deep ceramic sink with a traditional appearance, originally designed with an overflow at the front. It is heavier than a standard sink and requires a specially built or reinforced unit base to support it.
bodyHtml:
What Makes Belfast Sink Fitting Different

A Belfast sink is significantly heavier than a standard undermount or inset sink — typically 25–60kg depending on size. It must be supported on a dedicated base unit or custom-built support rather than simply resting on the worktop as a drop-in sink would. The worktop or unit front must be cut precisely to accept the sink apron.

Installation Steps

  • The base unit must be built or modified to provide solid support for the full weight of the sink
  • The worktop is cut to the correct depth to allow the sink to sit at the right height
  • The waste fitting and overflow (where applicable) are connected before the sink is positioned
  • Supply connections are made by a plumber after the sink is in place

Planning Considerations

Belfast sinks project further from the wall than standard sinks, which affects the worktop depth required. The waste position must also be planned carefully as the deep bowl creates a low outlet point. Discuss these requirements with your kitchen fitter and plumber at survey stage.

faqs.q: Can any kitchen unit support a Belfast sink?
faqs.a: No. A Belfast sink requires a reinforced or purpose-designed base unit. Standard pre-assembled base units may not be strong enough without modification.

faqs.q: Do I need a special tap for a Belfast sink?
faqs.a: Not necessarily, but the deep bowl of a Belfast sink means that a standard tap height may be impractical. A tall or crosshead tap is often chosen to suit the sink's depth and style.

faqs.q: How long does Belfast sink installation take?
faqs.a: Fitting a Belfast sink takes longer than a standard sink — typically 2–4 hours for the unit preparation and sink fitting, not including plumbing connection.

title: How Much Does Howdens Kitchen Installation Cost?
metaDescription: Find out the typical cost to have a Howdens kitchen professionally installed. Labour rates, what's included and how to get an accurate quote.
answer: Installation of a Howdens kitchen typically costs £1,500–£3,500 depending on kitchen size and complexity. This is labour only, excluding the Howdens units, worktops and appliances.
bodyHtml:
What Affects the Cost

  • Number of units — more units mean more installation time
  • Worktop type — laminate is cut on site; stone worktops are fitted separately after templating
  • Integrated appliances — each integrated appliance adds fitting time
  • Old kitchen removal — typically £200–£400 additional if included
  • Complexity — corner units, island units, unusual room conditions all add time

How to Get an Accurate Quote

A pre-installation survey from a specialist kitchen fitter is the only way to get an accurate price. The fitter assesses your room conditions, cross-checks your Howdens plan and provides a fixed price covering the full scope of work. Estimates without a site visit are unreliable.

What a Professional Installation Includes

A comprehensive Howdens installation quote typically includes all base and wall unit fitting, door and drawer alignment, worktop cutting and fitting, plinth and cornice fitting, integrated appliance installation, and a final snagging check.

faqs.q: Does Howdens offer installation?
faqs.a: Howdens is a trade-only supplier and does not offer a consumer installation service. You need to arrange your own independent kitchen fitter.

faqs.q: Can I get a cheaper installation for a Howdens kitchen?
faqs.a: Getting multiple fixed-price quotes after surveys is the best way to compare installation costs. The cheapest quote may not include all scope items — compare carefully.

faqs.q: Does Howdens kitchen installation include worktops?
faqs.a: The installation of worktops is separate from Howdens unit supply. If worktops are included in your installation quote, confirm exactly what is covered — fitting of laminate worktops differs from stone worktops, which require templating.

title: Can You Paint Kitchen Units Yourself?
metaDescription: Can you paint kitchen cabinet doors and units yourself? Find out how to do it properly and when to call a professional.
answer: Yes, kitchen units can be painted. It is possible to do it yourself but achieving a professional, durable result requires proper preparation, the right primer, and a kitchen-specific paint applied correctly. Many homeowners find a professional finish difficult to achieve without experience.
bodyHtml:
Can You DIY Paint Kitchen Units?

Yes, but the preparation is everything. Units must be fully degreased, sanded to provide a key, primed with the correct primer for the material (MDF, solid wood or vinyl-wrapped doors each require different primers) and painted in thin, even coats. Rushing the preparation produces a finish that chips and peels quickly.

Best Results: Spray Painting

A spray-painted finish gives the smoothest, most professional result on kitchen units. This is typically done by a professional kitchen painter using a spray gun. DIY brush and roller painting is possible but will show brush marks unless you use a very fine roller and finish with a tip-off brush technique.

When to Call a Professional

  • If your units are vinyl-wrapped — these require specific preparation and professional knowledge
  • If you want a factory-smooth finish without brush marks
  • If you have a large number of units and limited time

faqs.q: What paint is best for kitchen units?
faqs.a: A hard-wearing water-based alkyd paint (such as Farrow and Ball full gloss or Rust-Oleum kitchen cupboard paint) works well. Always prime first with an appropriate primer for the substrate.

faqs.q: How long does painted kitchen unit paint last?
faqs.a: With proper preparation and a quality paint, a painted finish can last 5–10 years. High-traffic areas (around handles and near the sink) will show wear first.

faqs.q: Is painting kitchen units worth it?
faqs.a: Yes — painting is one of the most cost-effective ways to transform a kitchen. New doors or a full kitchen replacement cost significantly more.

title: What Is a Correlated Kitchen?
metaDescription: A correlated kitchen coordinates all elements — doors, handles, worktops and appliances — to a unified design scheme. Find out what this means in practice.
answer: A correlated kitchen is one where all design elements — cabinet colours, door profiles, worktop material, handle style and sometimes appliances — are coordinated to a unified scheme. It is a design approach rather than a specific product category.
bodyHtml:
What Correlation Means in Kitchen Design

In kitchen design, correlation refers to the deliberate matching or complementing of all visual elements. A correlated kitchen has a clear design logic: the door colour relates to the worktop; the handle style reflects the door profile; the appliance finish matches the colour scheme. Nothing is arbitrary.

How It Affects Installation

A highly correlated kitchen design places greater demands on installation precision. When every element is designed to work together, misalignment is more visible — gaps between doors that are slightly inconsistent, a worktop that is a fraction out of level, or a plinth that doesn't sit flat all stand out more than they would in a less cohesive design.

Achieving a Correlated Look

  • Start with a consistent door colour and profile as the anchor for all other decisions
  • Choose worktop material and colour in person, not from a screen
  • Select handles that suit the door profile — bar handles for handleless-adjacent styles, cup handles for shaker

faqs.q: Is a correlated kitchen more expensive?
faqs.a: Not inherently. Correlation is a design principle, not a price point. You can achieve a well-coordinated kitchen at most budget levels with careful choices.

faqs.q: Does kitchen design correlation affect the fitter?
faqs.a: It places higher demands on installation quality — the fitter must achieve consistent door gaps, precise worktop joints and level plinths for the coordinated look to read correctly.

faqs.q: Do kitchen retailers offer correlated ranges?
faqs.a: Many do. Premium retailers like Magnet, Masterclass and German brands offer curated colour stories and accessory ranges designed to coordinate.

title: What Happens If a Kitchen Fitter Damages Something?
metaDescription: What are your rights if a kitchen fitter damages your property or kitchen during installation? Find out who is responsible and how to resolve it.
answer: If a kitchen fitter damages your property or kitchen units during installation, you are entitled to have the damage made good at the fitter's expense. A reputable fitter will have public liability insurance to cover accidental damage.
bodyHtml:
Your Rights

Under consumer law, if a tradesperson damages your property during the course of their work, they are responsible for repairing or replacing the damage. This applies whether the damage is to your property (walls, floors, existing fittings) or to items in their care (kitchen units, appliances).

What to Do If Damage Occurs

  • Document the damage immediately with photographs
  • Inform the kitchen fitter in writing (text or email creates a record)
  • Give the fitter the opportunity to rectify the problem before engaging other parties
  • If the fitter does not respond, contact their public liability insurer

Why Insurance Matters

A professional kitchen fitter should carry public liability insurance. This covers accidental damage to your property during their work. Always ask for proof of insurance before booking any tradesperson.

faqs.q: Can I withhold payment if a kitchen fitter damages something?
faqs.a: You can reasonably withhold a portion of payment that corresponds to the cost of making good the damage. Take legal advice if the fitter disputes liability.

faqs.q: What if a kitchen unit is damaged during installation?
faqs.a: If a unit is damaged during installation, the fitter is responsible for replacement. Document the damage and report it to the fitter immediately.

faqs.q: Does a kitchen fitter's insurance cover damage to new kitchen units?
faqs.a: Public liability insurance covers accidental damage to client property. Whether units are covered depends on the specific policy — check with the insurer.

title: How Do You Level a Kitchen on a Sloping Floor?
metaDescription: Sloping floors are common in older UK homes. Find out how professional kitchen fitters level units on uneven or sloping floors.
answer: Kitchen units are levelled on sloping floors using adjustable plinth legs. Each leg can be individually raised or lowered to compensate for floor variation, achieving a perfectly level unit regardless of what the floor does.
bodyHtml:
Plinth Legs

Modern kitchen units are fitted on adjustable plastic plinth legs (typically 100–150mm tall) that can be screwed up or down. By adjusting each leg independently, the fitter can level a unit across a floor with significant slope — typically up to 60mm variation — without shimming or cutting.

The Levelling Process

The fitter uses a spirit level (or laser level for larger runs) to establish the highest point in the floor. All units are then set to this level, with the legs on the lower parts of the floor adjusted upwards to compensate. The plinth panels that run along the bottom of the unit run conceal the legs and any floor variation behind them.

When Floors Are Very Severely Sloped

  • If floor variation exceeds the leg adjustment range, a levelling screed or floor build-up may be required before installation
  • Wall unit height must also be adjusted if floor slope affects ceiling clearance at one end of the kitchen
  • A survey is essential for identifying severe floor conditions in advance

faqs.q: How much floor slope can kitchen units handle?
faqs.a: Standard plinth legs accommodate floor variation of up to 60mm. Beyond this, floor preparation (screed or battening) is usually required.

faqs.q: Will a sloping floor affect the cost of installation?
faqs.a: A moderately sloping floor adds adjustment time but not usually significant cost. A severely sloped floor that requires preparation work will add cost — this will be identified at survey.

faqs.q: Do sloping floors affect worktop fitting?
faqs.a: The worktop sits on the unit tops which are levelled independently of the floor. Once units are level, the worktop line is level regardless of floor slope.

title: What Is the Best Kitchen Brand in the UK?
