metaDescription: Which is the best kitchen brand in the UK? From Wren and Howdens to Häcker and Neptune, find out how the top brands compare.
answer: The best kitchen brand depends on budget, design preference and how the kitchen will be used. Howdens and Wren dominate the mid-market; Häcker, Neptune and deVOL lead the premium market. All can be professionally installed to a high standard.
bodyHtml:
Mid-Market Leaders

Howdens is the UK's largest trade kitchen supplier, offering rigid units to trade customers through local depots. Wren is the largest consumer kitchen retailer, with showrooms nationally and a wide range of door styles. Both offer good quality for the price point.

Premium and Luxury Brands

Häcker (German) is widely regarded as the benchmark for precision-engineered, handleless kitchens. Neptune and deVOL offer high-quality British-designed kitchens in traditional and Shaker styles. Tom Howley and Harvey Jones produce bespoke in-frame kitchens at the top of the market.

What Matters Beyond Brand

  • The quality of the installation matters as much as the quality of the kitchen
  • A premium kitchen poorly fitted will not look or perform as it should
  • Choose a brand that suits your budget and appoint an experienced fitter with specific knowledge of that brand's system

faqs.q: Is Howdens better than Wren?
faqs.a: Howdens and Wren serve different markets. Howdens is trade-only and offers competitive pricing for rigid units; Wren is consumer-facing with an integrated showroom design service. Quality is comparable at similar price points.

faqs.q: Is a German kitchen worth the extra cost?
faqs.a: German kitchens (Häcker, Nobilia, Rotpunkt) typically offer superior unit construction tolerances and finish quality compared to mass-market UK alternatives. For many homeowners, the investment is justified by longevity and aesthetic precision.

faqs.q: What kitchen brand holds its value?
faqs.a: Premium kitchens from brands like Häcker, Neptune and deVOL hold their value better in property terms. A well-installed premium kitchen can add significant value to a home.

title: How Are Kitchen Appliances Measured for Fitting?
metaDescription: Kitchen appliances must be measured carefully to ensure they fit correctly into their housings. Find out how integrated appliances are measured and fitted.
answer: Kitchen appliances are measured by their standard niche dimensions — the space they need within the kitchen unit housing. Most integrated appliances are built to standard widths (450mm, 600mm) and heights (450mm, 820mm) that correspond to standard unit sizes.
bodyHtml:
Standard Appliance Dimensions

UK kitchen appliances are built to standard niche sizes. A standard dishwasher requires a 600mm wide by 820mm tall niche. A standard under-counter fridge requires a 600mm wide by 820mm tall niche. Built-in ovens typically require a 595mm wide by 590mm tall niche cut into a 600mm wide tower or base unit.

Checking Fit Before Installation

Your kitchen designer should specify the correct housing unit for each appliance in the plan. A pre-installation survey by your kitchen fitter will cross-check that the appliance dimensions match the specified housing and that the electrical supply point is in the correct position inside the housing.

What Goes Wrong When Measurements Are Wrong

  • An appliance that is slightly larger than the niche cannot be fitted without modification
  • A supply point in the wrong position inside a housing may prevent the appliance from sitting flush
  • Integrated doors that are the wrong height or offset will not align correctly

faqs.q: What if my appliance doesn't fit the housing?
faqs.a: If an appliance is too large for its housing, either a different appliance must be sourced or the housing unit must be changed. This is why checking appliance specs against housing dimensions before ordering is essential.

faqs.q: Do all 600mm wide dishwashers fit the same housing?
faqs.a: Generally yes — 600mm wide dishwashers are designed to fit a standard 600mm housing. Minor variations in height exist between brands; check the exact niche requirement in the installation manual.

faqs.q: Who checks appliance measurements — the fitter or the designer?
faqs.a: The kitchen designer specifies housing units based on appliance selection. The kitchen fitter verifies this at survey and again on delivery. Both should check — errors caught early are far cheaper to correct.

title: What Glue Do Kitchen Fitters Use?
metaDescription: Find out what adhesives and glues professional kitchen fitters use for worktops, end panels, plinths and other kitchen installation tasks.
answer: Kitchen fitters use several types of adhesive depending on the task. Worktop joints use two-part polyester adhesive (Biscuit); end panels and plinths use general construction adhesive (e.g. No More Nails or Gripfill); silicone sealant is used for sink surrounds and worktop-wall junctions.
bodyHtml:
Worktop Joint Adhesive

Worktop joints — particularly for laminate worktops — are joined using a two-part polyester adhesive specifically designed for worktops (commonly known as 'Biscuit' after the colour). This sets hard, is water-resistant and fills any minor gap in the joint. Routed joints (where a curved jig is used to create a perfectly matched joint profile) use the same adhesive.

Panel and Plinth Adhesive

For fixing end panels, light pelmet and decorative elements, a solvent-based or water-based construction adhesive is used alongside mechanical fixings. Products like Gripfill, No More Nails or Stixall are common. These provide a strong bond while remaining flexible enough to accommodate minor movement.

Silicone Sealant

Silicone is used for finishing joints between the worktop and wall, around the sink (where it also provides a waterproof seal) and in any other junction where flexibility and waterproofing are needed. Kitchen-grade silicone is anti-fungal to resist mould in humid conditions.

faqs.q: Do kitchen fitters glue units to the wall?
faqs.a: Not typically. Units are mechanically fixed to the wall using screws. Adhesive alone is not used to fix units.

faqs.q: What adhesive is used for kitchen end panels?
faqs.a: Construction adhesive (Gripfill, No More Nails or similar) combined with small fixings is typically used for end panels. Silicone may be used for the final seal at the wall junction.

faqs.q: Can I use silicone instead of worktop adhesive?
faqs.a: No. Silicone does not provide the rigid, load-bearing bond needed for a worktop joint. Two-part polyester adhesive is the correct material for worktop joining.

title: What Is a Handleless Kitchen Rail System?
metaDescription: A handleless rail system replaces traditional handles with an integrated grip rail. Find out how it works and what it requires from installation.
answer: A handleless rail system (also called a J-rail or aluminium rail system) replaces traditional pull handles with a recessed metal rail integrated into the door or a strip fitted between the door and the unit. It gives a clean, handle-free appearance while maintaining grip for opening.
bodyHtml:
How Rail Systems Work

In a J-rail system, a metal profile (typically aluminium) is fitted along the top and bottom of each door. The profile creates a recessed grip area. The rail can be mounted on the door face (surface mounted) or integrated into the door edge profile. When you open a door, your fingers fit into the rail rather than gripping a separate handle.

Installation Requirements

  • Handleless rail systems demand extremely precise door alignment — gaps must be consistent to within 1mm for the rail lines to run visually true across the kitchen
  • The rail is fixed to the door and to the unit frame — both must be exactly aligned for the integrated look to work
  • Hinge selection and adjustment is critical — hinges must hold the door in exactly the right position without drift

Why It Demands a Skilled Fitter

A handleless kitchen with rail system is one of the most demanding types to install precisely. The absence of handles means every millimetre of misalignment is visible. An experienced kitchen fitter will spend significant time adjusting door positions to achieve consistent rail lines across the entire kitchen.

faqs.q: Is a handleless kitchen harder to clean?
faqs.a: The recessed rail can collect grease and debris if not cleaned regularly. Wiping the inside of the rail as part of kitchen cleaning keeps it hygienic.

faqs.q: Does a handleless kitchen cost more to install?
faqs.a: Yes, typically — the precision required for consistent rail alignment adds installation time compared to a kitchen with traditional handles.

faqs.q: Which kitchen brands use rail systems?
faqs.a: Rail systems are used by most major handleless kitchen ranges including Wren's Infinity Plus, Howdens' Lincoln, Magnet's handleless ranges, and most German brands (Häcker, Nobilia, Rotpunkt).

title: Can Kitchen Units Be Reused?
metaDescription: Is it possible to reuse kitchen units after removal? Find out when reuse is practical and when replacement is the better option.
answer: Kitchen units can sometimes be reused, but it depends on their condition, the method of original installation and whether they can be safely removed without damage. Rigid pre-assembled units in good condition are the most likely candidates for reuse.
bodyHtml:
When Reuse Is Practical

Rigid kitchen units that were fitted with screws and are in good structural condition may be reusable. If the carcasse is sound, the hinge mounting positions are undamaged and the unit can be removed cleanly, it can be fitted in a new position. This is most common when rearranging a kitchen layout rather than a full replacement.

When Reuse Is Not Practical

  • Flatpack units (including IKEA) often cannot be disassembled and reassembled reliably — the joints weaken on removal
  • Units that were adhesive-bonded are unlikely to survive removal undamaged
  • Units with significant wear, water damage to the base or damaged hinge areas are not worth reusing
  • If the new layout requires different sizes, the existing units will not fit

New Doors on Old Units

A practical middle ground is to keep sound carcasses and fit new doors, drawer fronts and worktops. This can achieve a fresh kitchen look at significantly less cost than full replacement.

faqs.q: Can I donate old kitchen units?
faqs.a: Yes. Many local charities and organisations such as Habitat for Humanity or local Facebook groups accept kitchen units in good condition.

faqs.q: Can a kitchen fitter advise on reuse?
faqs.a: Yes — a good kitchen fitter will assess the existing units at survey stage and tell you honestly whether reuse is viable and worthwhile.

faqs.q: Is it cheaper to reuse kitchen units?
faqs.a: It can be — but only if the reused units are genuinely in good condition and the savings are not offset by additional fitting complexity.

title: How Do You Fit an Extractor Fan in a Kitchen?
metaDescription: How is a kitchen extractor fan installed? Find out what's involved in fitting a hood extractor, ceiling extractor or ducted extraction system.
answer: A kitchen extractor fan is fitted by securing the unit to a wall unit, island ceiling or dedicated mounting position, connecting it to a duct that exits through an external wall or roof, and wiring it to the electrical supply. Ducted extraction is preferred over recirculation for performance.
bodyHtml:
Types of Kitchen Extraction

Ducted extraction removes cooking fumes, steam and grease through a duct to the outside. It is the most effective system. Recirculation systems filter air through charcoal and return it to the room — no external duct is needed, but they are less effective at removing moisture and heat.

Installation Steps for a Ducted Extractor

  • Route the duct from the extractor position to an external exit point (usually through a wall behind the kitchen or upward through the ceiling)
  • Fit the duct grille on the external wall
  • Secure the extractor housing to the wall unit, ceiling or island support
  • Connect electrical supply (typically via a switched fused spur)
  • Connect the duct to the extractor

Who Does the Extractor Installation?

The kitchen fitter typically installs the extractor housing and duct within the kitchen. If the duct exits through an external wall, this element may require a builder or be done by the fitter if access allows. The electrical connection must be made by a qualified electrician.

faqs.q: Does a kitchen extractor need planning permission?
faqs.a: Not usually for a standard extraction to an external wall. Some installations (listed buildings, conservation areas, certain flat regulations) may require consent. Check with your local authority if in doubt.

faqs.q: How far can an extractor duct run?
faqs.a: Extractor ducts should be as short and straight as possible. Most extractor fans are rated for a maximum duct run of 3–6 metres with bends reducing effective extraction. Longer or more complex runs may require a higher-power fan.

faqs.q: Can an extractor fan be recirculating instead of ducted?
faqs.a: Yes, though performance is lower. Recirculating models are the only option where external extraction is not possible (e.g. in apartments or where the cooker is in the centre of a room with no nearby external wall).

title: What Is a Quartz Worktop?
metaDescription: Find out what a quartz worktop is, how it is made, and whether it is the right choice for your kitchen.
answer: A quartz worktop is an engineered stone surface made from approximately 90% natural quartz crystals bound with resins and pigments. It is non-porous, extremely hard-wearing and available in a wide range of colours and finishes.
bodyHtml:
How Quartz Worktops Are Made

Engineered quartz worktops are manufactured by combining ground quartz with polymer resins under high pressure. The result is a dense, non-porous surface that does not need sealing. Pigments are added during manufacturing to achieve consistent colour, making engineered quartz more predictable in appearance than natural granite.

Key Advantages

  • Non-porous — does not absorb liquids or stains
  • No sealing required
  • Consistent appearance (unlike natural stone where each slab is unique)
  • Extremely hard-wearing and scratch resistant
  • Hygienic and easy to clean

Limitations

  • Not suitable for outdoor use — UV exposure causes fading
  • Can be damaged by very high heat — use trivets or pan stands
  • Heavier than laminate, requiring strong unit tops
  • Cannot be cut on site — requires specialist templating and fabrication

faqs.q: Is quartz better than granite for a kitchen worktop?
faqs.a: Quartz is generally more practical — it requires no sealing, has consistent colour and is non-porous. Granite is natural stone with unique character but requires periodic sealing.

faqs.q: How much does a quartz worktop cost?
faqs.a: Quartz worktops typically cost £300–£900 per linear metre installed, depending on brand, thickness, edge profile and complexity.

faqs.q: Can quartz worktops be repaired if chipped?
faqs.a: Minor chips can be repaired using a colour-matched epoxy resin filler. Deep chips or cracks may require professional repair or section replacement.

title: How Do You Fit Kitchen Cabinets to Stud Walls?
metaDescription: Fitting kitchen cabinets to stud (plasterboard) walls requires the right fixings and technique. Find out how professional kitchen fitters do it.
answer: Kitchen cabinets are fitted to stud walls by locating the timber or metal studs using a stud finder, then fixing directly into the studs using appropriate screws. Where fixings cannot land in studs, specialist hollow wall anchors rated for the load are used.
bodyHtml:
Finding Studs

The first step is to locate the wall studs using an electronic stud finder. Studs in UK timber-frame walls are typically spaced at 400mm or 600mm centres. Once located, fixings can be driven directly through the plasterboard and into the stud for a secure, load-bearing connection.

When Studs Are Not in the Right Position

Wall units must be fixed at specific heights and positions to align with the kitchen plan. If a stud does not fall where a fixing is needed, the options are: (a) use a hollow wall anchor rated for the required load, (b) fit a horizontal batten across two or more studs and fix the unit to the batten, or (c) fit a full-width plywood backer panel behind the wall units.

Metal Stud Walls

Metal stud partitions are common in modern builds and some older properties. Fixings into metal studs require self-tapping screws and must be located precisely on the stud centre. Load capacity is limited compared to timber studs — heavy units may require a plywood backer for adequate support.

faqs.q: Can kitchen wall units be hung on plasterboard without studs?
faqs.a: Only with specialist hollow wall anchors rated for the load. Standard plasterboard fixings are not suitable for kitchen wall units. A professional will always try to fix into studs or use a batten system.

faqs.q: How much weight can a kitchen wall unit hold on a stud wall?
faqs.a: With fixings into timber studs, kitchen wall units can typically carry 50–100kg per cabinet. Hollow wall fixings carry less — always check the load rating against the anticipated cabinet weight.

faqs.q: Do I need to tell my kitchen fitter what type of wall I have?
faqs.a: It helps. Your kitchen fitter will establish wall construction at survey stage. Knowing in advance that you have stud walls allows them to plan fixings and bring appropriate equipment.

title: What Is a Pull-Out Kitchen Bin?
metaDescription: Pull-out kitchen bins are integrated waste solutions fitted inside kitchen cabinets. Find out how they work and how they are installed.
answer: A pull-out kitchen bin is a bin system built into a kitchen base unit that pulls out on a runner, typically attached to the door so it opens when you open the door. It conceals waste bins within the kitchen cabinetry for a tidy appearance.
bodyHtml:
How Pull-Out Bins Work

A pull-out bin unit consists of one or more bin containers mounted on a sliding frame inside a dedicated base cabinet. The door of the unit is either connected to the bin frame (so opening the door pulls out the bin automatically) or opens separately while the bin slides independently. Systems with door-linked pull-out action are the most popular as they require only one hand to operate.

Sizing and Cabinet Requirements

Pull-out bin systems are available in a range of widths — typically 300mm, 400mm, 450mm and 600mm. The system must be specified to match the available cabinet width. Some systems fit inside a single-door base unit; others require a wider two-door cabinet.

Installation Notes

  • The bin frame is fixed inside the base unit before the kitchen is installed, or as part of the kitchen fit
  • The door must be properly connected to the bin frame for the door-linked action to work
  • Once installed, door adjustment may be needed to ensure smooth, level operation

faqs.q: Can a pull-out bin be added to an existing kitchen?
faqs.a: Yes, if you have a suitably sized base unit. Pull-out bin systems are available as retrofit kits for units from 300mm wide upward.

faqs.q: How many bins fit in a pull-out unit?
faqs.a: Typically 1–3 bins depending on the system width. A 600mm wide system can accommodate a main waste bin and one or two recycling bins.

faqs.q: Are pull-out bins easy to clean?
faqs.a: Yes — the bin containers lift out for washing. The runners and frame can be wiped down with the unit open. Liner bags help keep the bins clean.

title: How Much Does German Kitchen Installation Cost?
metaDescription: German kitchen installation typically costs more than standard UK kitchen fitting due to precision requirements. Find out typical costs and what is included.
answer: Installing a German kitchen (Häcker, Nobilia, Rotpunkt, Pronorm) typically costs £2,500–£5,000 in labour, depending on the kitchen size and complexity. The precision demands of German kitchen systems add time and skill requirements compared to standard UK kitchen installation.
bodyHtml:
Why German Kitchen Installation Costs More

German kitchen brands — particularly Häcker — specify gap tolerances of 0.5mm between door faces. Achieving and maintaining this requires more time in adjustment, a more experienced fitter, and careful management of the installation environment (temperature and humidity affect engineered wood components). The result, when achieved, is visually exceptional — but it requires patience and precision that not every fitter can deliver.

What Affects the Cost

  • Number of units — larger kitchens take proportionally longer
  • Door type — handleless systems with rail profiles require more alignment time than handled doors
  • Worktop type — stone worktops add a template visit and separate fitting day
  • Appliance integration — German kitchens typically have more integrated appliances than standard UK kitchens

How to Find a Fitter for a German Kitchen

Ask the German kitchen supplier for recommended installers, or seek a kitchen fitter with verified experience with the specific brand. A fitter experienced in Häcker, for example, will know the unit assembly system, the hanging rail method and the adjustment mechanisms specific to that brand.

faqs.q: Does Häcker install their own kitchens?
faqs.a: Häcker and most German kitchen brands supply through approved UK retailers who arrange installation. Häcker does not directly employ fitters in the UK.

faqs.q: Is a German kitchen worth the investment?
faqs.a: For homeowners who value precision, longevity and design quality, yes. German kitchens typically use higher-grade materials and tighter manufacturing tolerances than mass-market UK alternatives.

faqs.q: Can any kitchen fitter install a German kitchen?
faqs.a: Technically yes, but achieving the specified gap tolerances and finish quality requires experience with the specific brand's unit system. Using an experienced fitter makes a significant difference to the final result.

Pindi Sahota at Install My Kitchen has 30 years of experience fitting kitchens across Coventry and Warwickshire.
bodyHtml:
What Makes a Kitchen Installer the Best?

The best kitchen installer is not simply the cheapest or the fastest. The defining qualities are: genuine hands-on experience (not subcontracted work), a thorough pre-installation survey, transparent fixed-price quoting, and a standard of finish that reflects the quality of the kitchen being installed.

Pindi Sahota — Install My Kitchen

Every installation is led by Pindi. He fits kitchens from all major retailers — Wren, Howdens, Magnet, IKEA, Benchmarx — and premium brands including Häcker, Nobilia, Neptune and deVOL.

What to Look For in a Coventry Kitchen Installer

  • Survey before every quote — no site visit means no accurate price
  • Fixed-price quote that does not change without a scope change
  • Identifiable experience with the specific kitchen brand you are buying
  • Public liability insurance and a clear snagging commitment

faqs.q: How do I find a kitchen installer in Coventry?
faqs.a: Ask your kitchen retailer for recommendations, search for locally-based specialist fitters, and always book a pre-installation survey before committing. Never accept a quote that has not been based on a site visit.

faqs.q: How do I book Pindi for a kitchen survey?
